coredata.dc:title: CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalyst for efficient visible light-driven purification of wastewater containing methyl orange dye
coredata.dc:creator: Shafique, Muhammad
Mahr, Muhammad Shabir
Yaseen, Muhammad
Bhatti, Haq Nawaz
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we report a cost effective and facile synthesis method for preparing carbon quantum dot incorporated TiO2 nanocomposite (CQD/TiO2) for rapid photodegradation of methyl orange (MO) under direct and indirect sun irradiations. In practice, TiO2 nanosols and CQD were prepared by sol-gel and hydrothermal routes, subsequently their nanocomposites (CQD/TiO2) by sonication assisted mixing process at ambient conditions. Different analyses like XRD, FTIR and SEM/EDX. TEM, XPS, Zeta-potential were employed to characterize the prepared catalysts. CQD/TiO2 prepared in the weight ratio of 1:10 of CQD and TiO2 exhibited optimized MO-photodegradation (three folded to the activity of pure TiO2) in direct sunlight exposures at pH 11. The results show that variations in the initial concentration and pH of MO solution as well as catalyst loadings have a significant impact on the photocatalytic performance of CQD/TiO2. Similar MO-percentage degradations for five consecutive cycles highlighted its long-term catalytic stability and good reusability potential. Because of good visible-light photocatalytic efficiency, profound catalytic-stability, and reusability for dyes photocatalysis, CQD/TiO2 nanohybrid is a potential candidate for photocatalytic purification of textile and other organic based wastewater under direct and indirect solar light exposures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Carbon quantum dot
Photocatalyst
TiO2
Catalytic support
Tauc plot
Methyl orange
Photocatalytic activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of aqueous TiO2 nano-sol and TiO2 nano-powder
Effect of initial pH of dye solution
Effect of amount of CQD in CQD/TiO2 composite
Effect of initial concentration of dye solution

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are highly thankful to Dr. Adil Murtaza of Xi'an Jiaotong University China for assisting in SEM/EDX, TEM and XPS measurements. Our research was supported from the general funds of University of Agriculture Faisalabad.

Authors are highly thankful to Dr. Adil Murtaza of Xi'an Jiaotong University China for assisting in SEM/EDX, TEM and XPS measurements. Our research was supported from the general funds of University of Agriculture Faisalabad .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of synthesized TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite.
FTIR of synthesized CQD, TiO2 and CQD/TiO2.
SEM images of synthesized TiO2 (a) synthesized CQD/TiO2 (b).
EDX spectrum for synthesized TiO2 (a) synthesized CQD/TiO2 (b).
TEM image of TiO2 (a) HRTEM image of TiO2 (b) TEM image of CQD/TiO2 (c) HRTEM image of CQD/TiO2 (d).
XPS spectra of CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite for full survey (a), C 1s (b), Ti 2p (c) and O 1s (d).
Zeta potential of CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite.
(a) UV–Vis absorbance spectra of synthesized TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite and (b) Tauc plots.
Visible absorption spectra of synthesized systems at different pH of dye solution (a) TiO2 (b) CQD/TiO2 and (c) dye degradation efficiency of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2.

(a) Effect of dopant content (CQD) in CQD/TiO2 nanocomposites. (The sample CT5 shows 1:5 ratio of C-dot:T-sol, CT10 shows 1:10 ratio of C-dot:T-sol, CT20 shows 1:20 ratio of C-dot:T-sol, CT30 shows 1:30 ratio of C-dot:T-sol, CT40 shows 1:40 ratio of C-dot:T-sol in CQD/TiO2 composites. All these five samples were calcinated at the same temperature 600 °C for 2 h. For all, MO concentration 20 mg/L at 11 pH, catalytic loading: 2mg/10 ml, direct sun light exposure time: 2h) and (b) Dye degradation efficiency.
Performance of CQD/TiO2 as visible light photocatalyst in light exposures (MO dye concentration: 20 mg/L at 11 pH, catalytic loading: 2mg/10 ml, direct sun light exposure time: 2h).
Variance of % deg and qe with catalyst dosage of CDQ/TiO2 in direct sunlight exposure (MO dye concentration: 20 mg/L at 11 pH, catalytic loading: 1 mg–20 mg, direct sun light exposure time: 2h).
Variance of % deg and qe with respect to concentration of MO dye solution (MO dye concentration: 5 mg/L to 100 mg/L at 11 pH, catalytic loading: 2mg/10 ml, direct sun light exposure time: 2h).

Effect of exposure time on % deg and qe values of optimized CDQ/TiO2 nanocomposite (MO dye concentration: 20 mg/L at 11 pH, catalytic loading: 2mg/10 ml of MO solution, direct sun light exposure time: 2h).

Photocatalytic stability of optimized CQD/TiO2 for five cycles in MO dye solution under visible light exposure (MO dye concentration: 20 mg/L at 11 pH, catalytic loading: 2mg/10 ml of MO solution, direct sun light exposure time: 2h). (a) Visible light absorption spectrum and (b) Percentage degradation of MO.

Photocatalytic degradation of MO by pure TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite (a) Percentage degradation with time, (b) Plot of Ct/Co versus time (c) Plot of ln(Ct/Co)versus time(MO dye concentration: 20 mg/L at 11 pH, catalytic loading: 2mg/10 ml of MO solution, direct sun light exposure time: 2h).
Kinetics of MO visible light photocatalytic reaction a) First order data fitting and finding R2 for TiO2b) First order data fitting and finding R2 for CQD/TiO2.
Kinetics of MO visible light photocatalytic reaction (a) Plot of 1/Ct versus time, (b) Second order data fitting and finding R2 for TiO2 and (c) Second order data fitting and finding R2 for CQD/TiO2.
Schematic diagram for the proposed mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of MO using CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite.
Reaction rate constants and regression coefficients (R2 values) for under investigated systems.

head.ce:title: CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalyst for efficient visible light-driven purification of wastewater containing methyl orange dye

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, University of Agriculture, Faisalabad, 38000, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, University of Agriculture, Faisalabad, 38000, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Agriculture, Faisalabad, 38000, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Agriculture, Faisalabad, 38000, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we report a cost effective and facile synthesis method for preparing carbon quantum dot incorporated TiO2 nanocomposite (CQD/TiO2) for rapid photodegradation of methyl orange (MO) under direct and indirect sun irradiations. In practice, TiO2 nanosols and CQD were prepared by sol-gel and hydrothermal routes, subsequently their nanocomposites (CQD/TiO2) by sonication assisted mixing process at ambient conditions. Different analyses like XRD, FTIR and SEM/EDX. TEM, XPS, Zeta-potential were employed to characterize the prepared catalysts. CQD/TiO2 prepared in the weight ratio of 1:10 of CQD and TiO2 exhibited optimized MO-photodegradation (three folded to the activity of pure TiO2) in direct sunlight exposures at pH 11. The results show that variations in the initial concentration and pH of MO solution as well as catalyst loadings have a significant impact on the photocatalytic performance of CQD/TiO2. Similar MO-percentage degradations for five consecutive cycles highlighted its long-term catalytic stability and good reusability potential. Because of good visible-light photocatalytic efficiency, profound catalytic-stability, and reusability for dyes photocatalysis, CQD/TiO2 nanohybrid is a potential candidate for photocatalytic purification of textile and other organic based wastewater under direct and indirect solar light exposures.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the top listed global challenges of 21st century is to overcome the energy crisis and to deplete pollution by employing low-cost approaches. Huge volume of wastewater from textile and other industries contains many toxic pollutants especially organic contaminants and synthetic dyes. Usually, this effluent is discharged into the water resources without proper treatment [1]. Development of removing dyes from wastewater is imperative than other colorless organic pollutants to avoid the environmental pollution as it affects more severely the aquatic ecosystem [2].The total solar energy absorbed by entire earth and its atmosphere is approximately 3.85 × 1024J per year that is infect so huge amount that its fraction absorbed in 1 h is sufficient for the whole world to use in one year [3,4].Thus, it is worthwhile to develop an effective and environmental-friendly technique that allows the entire range of solar spectrum to be used for resolving water pollution problems. Researchers have adopted numerous techniques and approaches to decontaminate polluted water. One of the efficient, cost-effective, and widely used approaches is heterogeneous photocatalysis under sun light exposure.

Photocatalyst is a substance that accelerates and enhances a light induced reaction without being consumed [5]. An ideal photocatalyst is activated by solar radiation (visible/invisible), nontoxic, chemically stable, easily available, low cost, and reusable as well as utilize a maximum range of solar spectrum [6]. Metal oxides semiconductors are frequently used as photocatalysts due to their suitable band gap for sun light [7].When a semiconductor material absorbs a photon having energy greater than or equal to the band gap energy then electron (e−)and hole (h+) pairs are created. This process of electron-hole pair generation by absorbing a photon of suitable energy is known as photoexcitation. Photoexcitation results in formation of reactive oxidizing species ( . OH, . O2 −) which can be used to decompose the organic contaminants in the waste water into mineral salts, CO2 and H2O [7–9].

Two primary factors should be considered while developing a system for photocatalytic process:(1) electron-hole pair production efficiency when the potential photocatalyst is exposed to sun light. (2) electron-hole pair separation efficiency before the loss of their redox ability due to recombination [5,7,10]. The efficiency of a photocatalyst can be improved significantly by: (i) increasing the absorbing range of solar spectrum for photocatalysis (ii) delaying or reducing the charge pair recombination. The most used physicochemical methods are less effective for the degradation of organic pollutants in the wastewater. Redox processes based on oxide semiconductor heterogeneous photocatalysts are adequately effective and well-suited techniques for the decomposition of organic compounds such as toxic dyes, with several widely reported oxide semiconductors such as TiO2, ZnS, WO3, ZnO, CdS, and Fe2O3 [13].

TiO2 is one of the most investigated photocatalyst due to its high thermal and chemical stability, low cost, less toxicity, good optical and thermal properties, reusability, highly photoactive and eco-friendliness [14–16]. One of the main drawbacks of TiO2 is that it can only be activated by UV radiation due to its large band gap (∼3.2 eV for anatase phase and ∼3.0 eV for rutile phase). UV radiation only composes 3–5% of the total photon flux reaching the earth surface whereas approximately 45% of the solar spectrum is in the visible range [32]. Another major disadvantage of TiO2 is its high electron-hole recombination rate, which dips the photocatalytic activity substantially.

The modification of TiO2 has been achieved by different processes such as coupling, doping, co-doping, and dye sensitization [17–20], heterojunction coupling [22,23] and by adding ions [24,25]. All these approaches reduce photo-corrosion rate consequently enhance photocatalytic activity of TiO2. Furthermore, doping with non-metals such as C, N, B, P, S, I and F have been shown improved results for visible light activity in TiO2 by reducing the band gap and increasing the lifetime of photo-generated charge carriers [8,26–28].

Herein, we reported a novel and facile synthesis method for preparing CQD/TiO2 nanocomposites and subsequently assessed the photocatalytic potential of the prepared systems by decomposing an aqueous methyl orange (MO) dye in the presence of direct and indirect low intensity sunlight. Influence of initial concentration of the dye solutions, pH of the dye solutions, CQD amounts in TiO2-nanocmposites, catalyst loadings and direct and indirect sunlight exposures on photocatalytic activity had been extensively investigated.

Titanium(IV) tetraisopropoxide (TIPT), acetylacetone(acac), acetic acid (AcOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), ascorbic acid, citric acid, sucrose, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethanol, methyl orange (MO) and distilled water. All the chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and were used as supplied without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of aqueous TiO2 nano-sol and TiO2 nano-powder
ce:section.ce:para: A simple sol gel method was used for the synthesis of aqueous TiO2 nano-sol as reported by Okunaka and his co-authors [29]. Following this approach, at room temperature, 20.0 mmol TIPT was added slowly to 20.0 mmol acac under continuous stirring, resulting into a yellow TIPT/acac solution. A 50 ml aqueous AcOH solution of 0.4 molL-1 was prepared separately. Subsequently, under continuous stirring, yellow TIPT/acac precursor was added dropwise to the aqueous solution of AcOH. When all the yellow solution was added to the aqueous AcOH solution, further stirring for 1 h was done to yield homogenous transparent titania sol. This sol will be denoted as T-sol hereafter. T-sol was dried at 60 °C in an oven; obtained solid matter (gel) was ground well and was calcinated at 600 °C for 2 h and white looked TiO2 powder was obtained as end-product. This white powder will be denoted as TiO2 hereafter.

To prepare CQDs, a facile hydrothermal method was used [42,66]. Following this process, 5 wt% of carbon precursors (ascorbic acid, citric acid, and sucrose in equal amounts) were mixed and dissolved in the mixture of water (60%) and ethanol (40%). This composite mixture was shaken well followed by sonication for 10 min. Subsequently, this mixture was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (a hydrothermal container). The autoclave was heated at constant temperature of 120 °C for 150 min and in subsequent step; this assembly was cooled down to room temperature. A transparent CQDs solution was obtained. It will be denoted as C-dots hereafter.

In this novel method, the prepared T-sol and C-dots were mixed in various ratios(v/v) in a beaker and the mixture was sonicated for 10 min, transferred in a petri dish for drying in an oven at 60 °C. The obtained crystals were ground well to get dull yellow powder. Upon calcination at 600 °C for 2 h sample was turned into white looked CQD/TiO2 nanocomposites.

Prior to evaluate the photocatalytic activities of prepared TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 samples, these were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Bruker D8 advance X-ray diffractometer to investigate the crystal structure/particle size, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using TEM, JEOL JEM-2100 to confirm the particle size and crystallinity, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) using SHIMADZU IRTracer-100 for the elucidation of structural features/optical properties, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using electron spectrometer (ESCALAB Xi, Thermo Fisher USA) to investigate the chemical composition of the samples, zeta-potential by zetasizer (Malven, UK) and Scanning electron microscopy/Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM/EDX) by using SEM, JEOL JSM-7000F to study the surface morphology/elemental compositions of the samples [13,21,28,30,31].

Photocatalytic potential of the prepared CQD/TiO2 nanocomposites was studied by degrading aqueous methyl orange dye (MO) in the presence of direct and indirect sunlight exposures in winter days when the temperatures were less than 15 °C and the intensity of sun light were significantly lower than normal having light intensity 76.2 mW/cm2 [11]. Methyl orange (MO) is an azo dye. Most of the azo dyes are extensively used in textile, pharmaceutical, printing and leather industries. It can enter through the human skin and can cause the death of lungs tissues, vomiting and fast heartbeat [32]. Being, highly toxic and relatively difficult to degrade, MO was selected as a model dye for photocatalytic degradation in this investigation. Photocatalytic degradation studies were carried out in 50 mL conical flasks by mixing 4 mg of the catalyst in 20 mL of aqueous methyl orange dye solution of defined concentration of 20 mg/L. The flasks were kept in the direct and indirect sunlight for predetermined time intervals. After this process, the solution was centrifuged and absorbance measurements were taken, for the residual dye, in the wavelength range of 400 nm–700 nm by using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (T80+ UV–Vis Spectrophotometer, PG Instruments limited, China). Photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO) at different time intervals was determined by finding the absorbance maxima (λmax = 485 nm).

Percentage degradation (% deg) of the dye was calculated by using equation (1) [30,33] and (2) [54–56]. Adsorption capacity ‘qe’ of the catalyst was determined by using equation (3) [2].
(1)
% deg =
(
A o
−
A t
)
× 100
A o
(2)
% deg =
(
C o
−
C t
)
× 100
C o
(3)
q e
=
(
C o
−
C t
)
×
V
( L )
m
( g )
where Ao (a.u) is the initial absorbance of the dye solution before the radiations exposure(at time t = 0) and At (a.u) is the final absorbance of the dye solution after exposure time ‘t’, qe (mg/g) is the amount of adsorbate (dye) taken up by the unit mass of adsorbent (the catalyst), Co(mg/L) is the initial concentration the dye solution before the radiations exposure(at time t = 0) and Ct(mg/L)is the final concentration of the dye solution after exposure time ‘t’ [47,56], V(L) is the volume of dye solution in liters, and m(g) is the mass of the catalyst used in grams [2].

To find the degradation reaction mechanisms, kinetics analysis of the degradation rates of methyl orange dye was studied by using synthesized TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite. Photocatalytic reaction kinetics study was preceded by finding order of the reaction and reaction rate constant for the synthesized photocatalysts [33,45]. First order and second order kinetics model were investigated to check the photocatalytic reaction kinetics of the dye.

As for low concentration of dye solution, the photocatalytic dye degradation can be illustrated by the Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics model [57]. Langmuir–Hinshelwood law simplifies to pseudo first order kinetic relation [58,59]:
(4) −dCt/dt = k1· Ct

And the relation for the second order kinetics model is given as [58,59]:
(5) −dCt/dt = k2· Ct 2

By integration, equations (4) and (5) become [58]:
(6)
C t
=
C 0
e
−
k
1
t
(7) 1/Ct = 1/C0 + k2t
Where C0 (mg/L) and Ct (mg/L) are the concentrations of the dye solution at time t = 0 and after time t and k1 and k2 are apparent first order and second order rate constants respectively and their values depends on dye concentration [33,60].

As equation (2.6) can be written as:
(8) ln(C0/Ct) = k1·t

Following equation (8), the values of k1 were found directly from the regression analysis of the linear curve in the plot ln (C0/Ct) versus t [60]. For second order kinetic model, the values of k2 were found from regression analysis of the linear curve in the plot l/Ct versus t [59].

The dye photocatalytic degradation experiments were run in triplicate and results were averaged. For statistical analysis, the regression coefficient (R2), the plotted graphs and required parameters were calculated using statistical functions of Origin program [2,61].

The XRD pattern of synthesized TiO2, calcinated at 600 °C and CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite is displayed in Fig. 1 . For both XRD radiographs, the peaks were high, narrow, and intense showing high crystallinity of the nanomaterials. Prominent peaks were appeared at 2θ values of 25.4°, 37.8°, 48.1°, 53.9°, 55.1°, 62.8° and 75.1° corresponded to crystal planes (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), and (107) respectively confirmed the anatase phase of pure TiO2 (JCPDS card 00-21-1272) [31,35].The peak positions at 27.5° and 54.4° for crystal planes (110) and (121) confirmed the rutile phase of TiO2 (JCPDS card 00-21-1276) as well [34,35].Similarly, for CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite, prominent peaks were appeared at 2θ values of 25.2°, 37.8°, 47.9°, 53.8°, 55.0°, 62.7° and 75.0° corresponded to crystal planes (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), and (107) respectively identified the anatase phase of pure TiO2 (JCPDS card 00-21-1272) [31,35].A single prominent peak positioned at 2θ value of 27.4° with crystal plane (110) confirmed the existence of rutile phase of TiO2 (JCPDS card 00-21-1276) [34,35].The XRD pattern for CQD/TiO2 was like TiO2 pattern indicating that crystal structure of TiO2 was maintained in the composite.

For both the samples, anatase phase characteristic peaks were high and greater in numbers showing the dominancy of this phase and high crystallinity whereas the peaks associated to rutile phase were fewer and shorter indicating low fraction and crystallinity of that phase. No peak could be appeared for CQDs in the CQD/TiO2 pattern because CQD is amorphous, and its quantity was too low in the composite to be detected by XRD [31]. Crystallite sizes for three highest peaks in both samples were calculated by Debye-Scherrer formula.
(9)
D =
K λ
β cos θ
Where, K is Scherrer constant having value 0.9, λ is wavelength of X-ray used with value 0.15406 nm, β is FWHM (full width at half maximum) and θ is diffraction angle [35–37]. The particle sizes for pure TiO2 were found to be 27.8 nm, 26.0 nm and 27.3 nm (average size = 27.0 nm) and for CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite were 25.7 nm, 24.1 nm and 25.3 nm (average size = 25.0 nm). These calculated particulate dimensions indicated that particles are of uniform sizes for both the samples. Further, a little decrease in particle size of the nanocomposite was noted that caused an increase in the surface area of the photocatalyst thereby offering more interaction of photon to the catalyst atoms producing more active species to degrade the dye molecules and ultimately the rate of photocatalytic reaction was increased substantially.

To investigate the absorption bands and the related functional groups in CQD, TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 samples, FTIR analysis was carried out and the spectra is displayed in Fig. 2 . FTIR spectra of all three materials showed a broad band at 3200-3500 cm−1 which is associated to O–H stretching vibrations of the surface adsorbed water [38,97]. Absorption bands at 2982 cm−1 and 2838 cm−1 were found for both CQD and CQD/TiO2 spectra attributed to C–H2 aliphatic asymmetric and aliphatic symmetric stretching vibrations respectively confirmed the presence of CQDs in the composite [39,59].

The peaks at around 2360 cm−1 in all the three spectra was corresponded to O–H stretch from strongly hydrogen-bonded and the band at 1560-1680 cm−1 was associated to H–O–H bending vibration showing the presence of many -OH groups in the samples. [13,97,103]. The intense absorbance peak at 1016 cm−1 in CQD spectra, a less intense peak at 1020 cm−1 in CQD-TiO2 and a peak at 1021 cm−1 in TiO2 are relevant to C–O–C, Ti–O–C and Ti–O–Ti bonds respectively [39,97]; which agrees with the XPS results [Fig. 6]. In addition, A broad band below 940 cm−1 in the spectra of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 samples attributing Ti–O stretching vibrations further affirmed the existence of TiO2 in the samples [13,38,40].

SEM analysis was used to investigate the surface morphology of synthesized TiO2 and CD/TiO2 nanocomposite. In pristine TiO2 SEM image, nanoparticles were spherical in shape [33,49](Fig. 3 (a)). But in CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite, due to agglomeration, the shape was nearly spherical (Fig. 3(b)) and it is difficult to obtain the exact morphology of the composite [39]. The elemental compositions of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 samples were determined by EDX spectrum shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (b) respectively. Fig. 3(a) and (b) also represent the selected samples area from which the EDX was obtained. Fig. 4(a) confirmed the presence of Ti and O elements in TiO2 and Fig. 4(b) illustrated the presence of C, Ti and O in CQD/TiO2 composite. The elemental peaks of Na and Cl showed small impurities in the CQD/TiO2 composite sample.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analyses were used to investigate the morphology and microstructures [12] of the synthesized TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite. Low magnification TEM image of synthesized TiO2 is displayed in Fig. 5
(a) and CQD/TiO2 image is shown in Fig. 5 (c). High resolution TEM images of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 are placed in Fig. 5(b) and (d) respectively. TEM images show that TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 nanoparticles are in a high-quality of crystallinity and fine ordered structures [98–100]. TEM micrographs of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 reveal that these are approximately spherical in shapes and in uniform particle sizes very similar information extracted from SEM analysis. The particle sizes are seemed quite in agreement with those calculated in XRD analysis (25 nm for TiO2 and 27 nm for CQD/TiO2) [39,101]. HRTEM images of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 exhibit well-resolved lattice structure and uniform interplanar spacing of the synthesized TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite. The dark spots in the TEM images of CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite represent the presence of CQD on the surface of TiO2 [38]. This is the confirmation of heterostructure, which is suited to charge separation and increases the electron transfer efficiency within this hybrid structure and resultantly increased the photocatalytic performance of CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite in relation to pristine TiO2 nanoparticles [102].

To investigate the chemical compositions and states of synthesized CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used. The full survey spectrum and narrow scan XPS patterns for the CQD/TiO2 composite is shown in Fig. 6. The intense peaks in the survey spectrum around 284, 458 and 528.4 eV confirmed the presence of carbon (C 1s), titanium (Ti 2p) and oxygen (O 1s) respectively in the composite [96].

In the narrow scan of C 1s spectrum (Fig. 6b), the main peak appeared at 283.4 eV is attributed to the CC sp2 orbital and the peak with binding energy 287 eV is assigned to sp3 hybridized carbons [95]. Fig. 6(c) shows the narrow scan Ti 2p spectrum with peaks at 457.3 and 463 eV pointing out the presence of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 respectively; representing that titanium metal is there in Ti (IV) state in CQD/TiO2 composite. The results show that there is no change in the binding energies of C 1s and Ti 2p which is obvious from the reported literature, so it can be concluded that in the formation of CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite, CQD are merely adsorbed on TiO2 surface and there is no chemical bonding exists between them [96]. Similarly, the intense peak of O 1s in the survey spectrum of the composite can be extended into two peaks at 528.6 eV and 530.8 eV [109], recognized to TiO, CO respectively [Fig. 6d]. Both C 1s and O 1s spectra indicate the presence of carbon-oxygen bonds in CQD/TiO2 composite. This extended XPS pattern of O 1s is evident to form Ti–O–C bonds in the composite, which agrees with the FTIR results (Fig. 2) [53,95,96].

Surface charge or zeta potential of CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite is displayed in Fig. 7 . Double peak zeta potential was observed for acidic, neutral, and basic aqueous MO CQD/TiO2 dispersions. Since, CQD/TiO2 is a composite composed of TiO2 and CQDs components with different particle size distributions thereby each one contributes to its own zeta-potential. By increasing pH, zeta potential of TiO2 component was shifted from negative to positive (-34.05 mV at pH3 to 29.38 mV at pH 11). Similar shift towards positive zeta potentials was noted for CQD component of CQD/TiO2 nanohybrid upon increasing pH. These zeta-potential results indicate the stability of CQD/TiO2 in the dispersions because of electrostatic repulsion caused by like charges of composite.

Optical properties of synthesized TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 nano-systems were studied by UV–Vis spectroscopy. The absorbance spectra of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 8 (a).It is observed that the absorption edge of the TiO2 sample lies at 387 nm and almost no absorption of visible photons showing large energy band gap lying in the UV region of the solar light [60,63,64].Intrinsic band gap absorption of TiO2 by absorbing UV photons from sunlight, resulting in the excitation of electrons from valence band to the conduction band [67]. However, it can be observed that there is broadening of the absorption band of CQD/TiO2 composite and absorption edge lies at 419 nm of the solar spectrum showing the red shift of the absorption edge as compared to pure TiO2. This red shifting of absorption edge confirmed the reduction of band gap for CQD/TiO2 sample (2.96 eV) compared with TiO2 sample (3.2 eV) as shown in Fig. 8(b) and indicating the chemical bonding between TiO2 and specific sites of carbon atoms [68]. Due to this broadening of absorption band of the solar spectrum for CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite as compared to TiO2 indicated the improved solar energy utilization efficiency of the CQD/TiO2 photocatalyst, resulting in the formation of greater number of electron-hole pairs and hence enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [69].

Direct optical band gap of the samples was calculated by using Tauc's equation (10) [59].
(10) α = k (hυ – Eg)1/2/hν
where ‘α’ is the absorption co-efficient, ‘Eg’ is the band gap energy, ‘k’ is the constant and ‘hν’ is the photon energy.

The value of ‘α’ is given below in equation (11) [70].
(11) α = 2.303 × 103Aβ/lC
where ‘A’ is the absorbance of the sample, ‘β’is the density of the TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 composite, l is the path length of the cuvette cell (1.0 cm), and C is the concentration of the photocatalyst in the suspension.

Extrapolation of the linear portion of (αhυ)2 versus hν curve helped in determining of the band gap and the intercept of hν axis gives the value of energy band gap as shown in Fig. 8 (b). This figure 7(b) shows that the optical band gap of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite are 3.2 eV and 2.96 eV respectively. The decrease in band gap from 3.2 eV to 2.96 eV, implies that absorption of more photons in the visible region and expected the formation of greater number of photo-generated electron-hole pairs thereby enhanced photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst [59,71].

Photocatalytic potential of the synthesized nanomaterials (TiO2 and CDQ/TiO2) was investigated by studying different parameters such as effect of initial pH of dye solution, CQD concentration (dopant content) in the composite, stability/reusability of the CQD/TiO2 photocatalyst, effect of direct/indirect light exposures on photocatalytic activity, effect of catalyst without radiation, effect of catalyst dose, effect of initial concentration of dye solution, effect of exposure time and reaction kinetics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial pH of dye solution
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of effluent of textile industry varies with the chemical compounds added to the dye during processing and type of dyes used [44]. The effect of pH of MO solution is considered as a main factor on the photocatalytic degradation of the dye solution. The pH of the dye solution was adjusted by using HCl and NaOH solutions, while catalyst dose (2mg/10 ml), dye concentration (20 mg/L) and exposure time (2 h) were kept constant.

Photocatalytic results are displayed in Fig. 9 . The results showed that the efficiency of synthesized materials (both TiO2 and CQD/TiO2)on the photodegradation of the dye was affected by varying pH of the MO solution. The results obtained showed that both TiO2 and CQD/TiO2are active in acidic (pH 3), neutral (pH 7) and basic (pH 11) media for aqueous methyl orange solution under direct sunlight (Fig. 9(a) and (b)) but both materials showed highest activity in basic pH 11 with 24.2% and 71.9% degradation of the dye respectively (Fig. 9(c)).At pH 11, CQD/TiO2 showed 3-fold (200% increased) efficiency as compared to pure TiO2 under direct sunlight. For the sake of comparison of the performance of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 as visible light photocatalysts for the photodegradation of MO in the three different media is shown in Fig. 9(c). Results reveal that the medium pH is a critical parameter for the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst.

It is also reported that photodegradation rates for MO is low in the acidic ranges of aqueous solution. Although at pH 5 dye degradation was observed maximum. It is due to the fact that in less acidic condition the formation of hydroxyl radicals ( . OH) is extensively increased which in turn enhanced the catalytic activity. On the other hand, in high acidic range of pH, the production of hydroxyl radicals decreased and hence less photocatalytic activity [49–51]. It should also be noted that in low pH solutions, catalyst particles try to agglomerate causing a decrease in the surface area of the catalyst particles and consequently sites for photon absorption and dye adsorption decreased [41].

Photocatalytic decomposition efficiency was also expected to increase with the increase of initial pH value due to the fact that by trapping photoinduced holes, the hydroxyl radical ( . OH) concentration increases on the surface of the catalyst (TiO2and CQD/TiO2)in alkaline medium [43,44]. The resulting ( . OH) radicals, being very strong oxidizing agents (redox potential +2.8 V) oxidized most of methyl orange dye into mineral salts, CO2 and H2O end-products [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of amount of CQD in CQD/TiO2 composite
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of CQD in CQD/TiO2 on the visible light photocatalytic performance is demonstrated in Fig. 10 . It is observed that by increasing amount of CQD in the composite, degradation efficiency increases and for suitable ratio of 1:10 (C-dot:T-sol) optimum photocatalytic efficiency was yielded. Further inc

rease in CQDs decrease the efficiency of the nanocomposite (Fig. 10(b)). It is because, at low concentration of CQDs in the CQD/TiO2nanocomposite, less photo-generated electrons were accommodated by carbon atoms and there was more charge pair recombination and resultantly low photocatalytic efficiency occurred (Fig. 10(b)). Since, carbon atoms absorb visible light, for higher content of CQDs in the composite (1:5) results in a competition of visible light absorption and availability of visible light photons to produce electron-hole pairs decreases and hence degradation of MO is decreased [52,72].

Furthermore, when dopant concentration increases, it decreases the particle exposed surface area of the photocatalyst (TiO2), consequently reduces the adsorption of reactant resulting in the slowing down the activity of the catalysts [74]. When amount of dopant added in excess, TiO2 surface does not exposed to the incident radiations which impede the photoexcitation and minimizing the generation of electron-hole pairs consequently reduces the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst [75].

The synthesized CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite showed photocatalytic activity in both direct and indirect (in the shadow) sunlight with 71.9% and 45.8% degradation respectively as shown in Fig. 11 . In indirect light, intensity of photons is less than the direct light intensity decreasing the production of electron-hole pairs and subsequently decreasing the reactive species ( . OH, . O2 −). The degradation rate increases with the increase of intensity of light up to a certain limit [43].

Effect of catalytic loading on photocatalytic activity of CQD/TiO2 is displayed in Fig. 12 . It was observed that percentage degradation (% deg.) of the dye significantly depends upon the catalyst dose [33,47]. The results showed that percentage removal of the dye was increased by increasing the catalyst dose from 1 mg to 15 mg and become constant from 15 mg to 20 mg (Fig. 12).

The percentage degradation was 48.2% for 1 mg dose and increased to 99.4% for 20 mg dose of the catalyst. The optimum percentage degradation was observed for 15 mg/10 ml loading of the catalyst. The percent degradation of the dyes by increasing the catalyst dosage might be due to the increase of photo-generated electron-hole pairs which in turn increase the number of hydroxyl and superoxide radicals [59,71,76]. In fact, an increase in the amount of catalyst increases the number of active sites on the photocatalyst surface and consequently improves the photoreaction rates [77,78]. It was also observed that the reaction rate increases with the concentration of catalyst until a maximum constant value corresponding to the total absorption, above this limit of concentration of the catalyst, the photocatalysis rate will even decrease due to the interception of the light by the suspension [7,79]. Moreover, with the increase of catalyst dose above the optimum value may result in the agglomeration of catalyst's particles and hence the part of the catalyst surface become opaque for photon absorption thereby photocatalytic degradation rates drop off [7,76,80–84].

Adsorption capacities (qe) were also determined for the same samples of MO (Fig. 12). It was observed that adsorption capacity of the catalyst significantly depends upon the catalyst dosage [2]. The results revealed that adsorption capacity decreased significantly by increasing the catalyst dose from 1 mg to 9 mg/10 ml. However, for catalyst doses, beyond 9 mg up to 20 mg per10 ml, the qe values decreased insignificantly. The adsorption capacity was 96.4 mg/g at 1 mg per 10 ml catalyst dose. The adsorption capacity was decreased to 9.9 mg/g at 20 mg dose of the catalyst (Fig. 12). The decrease in the adsorption capacity by the increase of catalyst dose was due to higher available surface area at lower dosage and more active sites of the catalyst were available for the dye molecules [85]. However, the decrease in the qe values for higher dose of the catalyst at constant volume and concentration of the dye solution, net surface area might be decrease due to aggregates formation consequently decrease the active sites of the catalyst for the dye molecules [86,87].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial concentration of dye solution
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of dye degradation efficiency on MO dye solution initial concentration is demonstrated in Fig. 13 . It was observed that percentage degradation of the dye significantly depends on the concentration of the dye solution. The results showed that percentage removal was decreased by increasing the concentration of the dye solution.

The percentage degradation was 90.3% at 5 mg/L and decreased to 60.8% for 100 mg/L concentration of the dye solution. All the molecules of the dye do not decompose, only those molecules of the pollutant degrade which are adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface [88]. The number of pollutant molecules increases with the increase of the concentration of the dye in the solution, as a result degradation rate was affected. Therefore, concentration of the dye in the solution should not be increased above a certain limit for the optimum degradation rate [89]. Photocatalytic degradation process proceeds slowly with the increase of initial concentration of the dye solution. It is because more dye molecules adsorbed on the active sites of catalyst surface thereby generation of reactive species (hydroxyl and superoxide radicals) at surface of the catalyst reduced [90–92].

Adsorption capacities (qe) were also found for the same samples of MO aqueous solutions (Fig. 13). It was observed that adsorption capacity of the catalyst significantly depends on the concentration of the dye solutions [2,93]. The results showed that adsorption capacity increased significantly by increasing the concentration of dye solution. Adsorption capacity was 22.6 mg/g at 5 mg/L concentration of the solution and increased to 304 mg/g for 100 mg/L concentration solution (Fig. 13). At lower concentration of the dye solution at constant volume and dose of the catalyst adsorption sites were available for dye molecules. With the increase of concentration of the dye solution, more dye molecules adsorbed on the sorption sites of the catalyst [2,73,94].

Percentage degradations and adsorption capacities (qe) of the dye solution were calculated for each sample exposed of optimized CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite for different time intervals and were displayed in Fig. 14 . Results indicated that percentage degradations and adsorption capacities were exponentially increased with the increase of exposure time. A longer illumination time increased the interaction probability of dye molecules, and the catalyst particle surfaces; consequently, percentage degradations and adsorption capacities were increased with the increase of exposure time very much in accordance with literature [46,49]. Percent degradation and adsorption capacity of the catalyst were found to be 82.5% and 82.5 mg/g respectively after 180 min irradiation time.

The rate of photocatalytic degradation of the dye depends on the irradiation time as well [91]. It is observed that the rate of percentage degradation and adsorption capacity were higher at the beginning, but later catalytic process was slowed down with the passage of time. Percent degradation and adsorption capacity become nearly constant in the last 30–40 min showing that the reaction rate retards with the increase of exposure time very similarly as indicated by Konstantinou and Albanis [48]. At the start of the photocatalytic process, reaction proceeds fast due to excess of the active sites availability on the catalyst surface as well as high rate of production of electron-hole pairs. For the longer exposure time, however byproducts are collected on the active sites of photocatalyst surface leading deactivation of catalyst surface Furthermore, for longer exposure times, electron hole pair recombination increases thereby depleting the photocatalytic process [55].

Lifetime of a photocatalyst is a significant factor in determining the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the photocatalyst, as considerable cost reduction can be achieved if the photocatalyst can be used for many cycles and for longer period [44]. Photocatalytic performance of CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite in five cycles is shown in Fig. 15 . Results of five cycles showed that 71.9% of the dye solution was degraded in first cycle and 64.1% for the fifth cycle (just about 8.5% decrease was occurred in five cycles) exhibiting good reusability of the synthesized CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite (Fig. 15(b)). Slightly decreased photocatalytic efficiency in five cycles might be due to the loss of some active sites of the catalyst surface by localization of produced byproducts over it, causing reduction of incident photons collection efficiency of the catalyst [49,62]. Consequently, photogenerated electron-hole pairs reduced which reduced the hydroxyl radicals and resultantly degradation efficiency of the catalyst was slightly decreased [65].

Time dependent photocatalytic degradation behavior of MO dye by pure TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 was displayed in Fig. 16 (a), (b) and (c). For first order kinetic model, following equation (8), the values of k1for TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 photocatalysts were found directly from the regression analysis of the linear curve in the plot ln(C0/Ct) versus t [60,110] as shown in Fig. 17 (a)and(b) respectively. For second order kinetic model, the values of k2for TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 were found from regression analysis of the linear curve in the plot l/Ct versus t [59] as shown in Fig. 18 (b) and (c) respectively. Correlation coefficient (R2) for two curves decided that which of the two models obeyed for the degradation of the dyes. Reaction rate constants k1 and k2 and corresponding R2 values for first order and second order kinetics models are shown in Table 1 .

By comparing the correlation coefficients (R2) it can be concluded that the photocatalytic degradation reactions of MO using TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 are fitted more accurately with the second order kinetic model than with the first order kinetic model which a similar kinetic behavior of these composite systems as that TiO2/CNT based nanocomposites [59].

The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite can be understood from the sketch demonstrated in Fig. 19
. When suspended CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite in the aqueous dye solution absorbed photons of energy (hν) greater than or equal to band gap energy, conduction band free electrons (e − ) and valence band holes (h+) are generated. These free electrons react with O2 absorbed on the photocatalysts surface or dissolved in water reducing into superoxide radical anion O2
·− [104] and holes react with OH− or H2O oxidizing them to form extremely powerful hydroxyl radicals . OH as oxidants (with redox potential +2.80 V). Along with other reactive species (peroxide radicals), h+, . OH and O2
·− subsequently decompose the dye molecules into mineral salts, CO2 and H2O end products [8,45,106].

Typically, photogenerated charge carriers (conduction band free electrons ‘e − ’ and valence band holes ‘h+’) rapidly recombine and only a fraction of e− and h+ can take part in the photocatalytic degradation process, resulting in low efficiency [105]. However, when TiO2 was modified by CQD (as a catalytic support), the photocatalytic activity of the CQD/TiO2 composite was increased [47,107]. The transfer of free electron (e − ) from the TiO2 to CQD surface is supposed to enhance OH radical formation and hence, resulting a higher photocatalytic activity. Second possibility is the direct transfer of the hole (h+) to the dye molecule to oxidize it [108]. In both the cases CQD played the role to increase the charge transfer from the photocatalyst [13,47].

TiO2 nanoparticles and CQD/TiO2 nanocomposites were successfully synthesized by sol gel and sonication mixing processes, respectively. Both systems had spherical morphologies with average particles of 27 nm for TiO2 NPs and 25 nm for CQD/TiO2. CQD/TiO2 was photoactive in all three acidic, natural, and basic media however it yielded optimum photocatalytic activity at pH 11 that was three folded (200%) to the activity of pure TiO2. This enhancement may be due to reduction of band gap of the composite (2.96 eV from 3.2 eV) to improve interfacial charge transfer at the interface of CQDs and TiO2 that might decease the photocorrosion resulting more availability of active species for demolishing dye molecules. An increase of surface area of the nanocomposite due to decrease in particulate size may cause light exposure of a greater number of atoms of the photocatalyst thereby enhances MO-photodegradation. Further optimizations suggest that a CQD/TiO2 prepared in the ratio of 1:10 of CQD and TiO2 exhibited optimized photodegradation of MO in direct sunlight exposures with a loading dose of 15 mg per 10 ml of MO dye aqueous solution with initial MO-concentration of 20 ppm. Similar MO percentage degradations for 5 consecutive cycles highlighted its long-term catalytic stability and good reusability potential. First order and second order kinetic models were well validated for both systems; however, second order kinetic model was more accurately fitted for both pristine TiO2 and CQD/TiO2.

Muhammad Shafique: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Muhammad Shabir Mahr: Writing – original draft. Muhammad Yaseen: Data, Formal analysis, Data curation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Draft reviewing and editing. Haq Nawaz Bhatti: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are highly thankful to Dr. Adil Murtaza of Xi'an Jiaotong University China for assisting in SEM/EDX, TEM and XPS measurements. Our research was supported from the general funds of University of Agriculture Faisalabad.

I. Ullah, R. Nadeem, M. Iqbal, Q. Manzoor, Ecol. Engg. 60 (2013) 99-107.
